Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd sit down and study, but then hours would pass and I'd realize I'd been doing other things. At 29, I've got a lot going on outside of school, and studying just kept getting pushed to the back burner. I'd maybe spend a couple hours here and there, but it was scattered and unfocused most of the time.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd have these bursts where I'd try to catch up before a deadline, but then I'd go days without touching anything course-related. There was no real consistency week to week. Life stuff kept getting in the way, and I just couldn't lock into a rhythm no matter how many times I tried to set one up.
Q3: Usually it was guilt or panic that got me to log in. I'd remember I hadn't checked the LMS in a while and feel this wave of anxiety about what I might be missing. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a deadline and that would push me to log in. It wasn't really a proactive thing for me—more reactive, like damage control.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. I tried to make it a habit early on, but it never stuck. I'd log in, feel overwhelmed by everything I needed to do, and then close it. The routine never developed because every time I logged in, it reminded me how behind I was, which just made me want to avoid it more.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful when I actually watched them. I could pause and rewind, which helped since my mind tends to wander. The discussion boards were okay too—seeing other students' questions made me feel less alone in my confusion. Reading through those helped me understand concepts I'd missed.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the supplementary readings and optional materials. There was just so much content, and I was already struggling to keep up with the basics. The extra stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't afford when I was barely managing the required work. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never came.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to passing the course. If it was going to help me on an assignment or exam, I'd make an effort. But if something felt theoretical or like busy work, I just couldn't motivate myself to engage with it. I needed to see the point right away.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. Required ones at least had the threat of a grade attached, which gave me some external push. Without that pressure, optional stuff just fell off my radar entirely. I know that's not the best approach, but when you're already struggling, you triage what you can.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download PDFs and readings to my tablet and study from there. I also used some YouTube videos to explain concepts the course materials didn't make clear to me. I have decent resources, so I'd sometimes buy additional textbooks or find external articles that explained things in ways that clicked better for me.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be working through problems on paper or re-reading downloaded notes without ever touching the LMS. I'd sit at coffee shops with my materials spread out, genuinely trying to understand things, but none of that would show up in any log. The LMS only saw me when I logged in, not when I was actually wrestling with the content.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or PDF once, and then that became my study material for days. I didn't see the point in streaming the same video multiple times when I could just have it on my device. So my one download might have represented hours of actual engagement that the system never captured.
Q12: Around midterms, everything just felt impossible. My anxiety spiked really high, and even though I usually handle stress okay, the combination of falling behind and seeing my grades slip made me want to shut down. I went through a CBT session which helped a bit, but there were weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to face the course.
Q13: Absolutely. I've got responsibilities outside of school that a lot of younger students don't have. When life got demanding, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Fatigue was a big one too—after a long day, the last thing I wanted was to log in and face how much work I'd been avoiding. The confusion about certain topics made it worse because I didn't even know where to start.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open up materials and just stare at them, not really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere—worrying about other things, feeling sad about how the semester was going. I'd technically be 'studying' but I wasn't actually learning. It was like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety would build up around them until they felt insurmountable. It's like the more important something was, the scarier it became to actually start it. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same excuse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd see a deadline approaching and finally force myself to engage. But by then, I usually didn't have enough time to do quality work. It was always a last-minute scramble, which probably explains my low assignment completion rate despite decent scores when I did submit.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it was a mixed bag. Good feedback motivated me briefly, but critical feedback often made me spiral a bit. I'd feel like I was failing and that made me want to disengage more. The CBT sessions helped me process that better, but it was still hard not to take negative feedback personally.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with six online courses at once. There was so much content spread across different modules that I never felt like I had a clear path forward. I needed more guidance on prioritization, but the structure assumed I'd figure it out myself. That assumption didn't work well for someone like me who was already struggling with motivation.
Q19: The sheer volume of content made engagement harder. When everything feels equally important, nothing feels manageable. What would have helped is clearer signposting—like 'focus on this first' or 'this is essential vs. supplementary.' The discussion forums were actually designed well and made engagement easier when I used them.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. You can spend a lot of time in the LMS reading discussions, watching videos, trying to understand things, but then when it comes to actually producing work, you freeze up. The engagement is there mentally, but the output isn't. Anxiety can create this gap between consuming content and demonstrating knowledge.
Q21: I've seen classmates who barely log in but ace everything. Some people just learn differently or have strong backgrounds in the subject. For me, low activity usually meant low performance, but I can see how someone who's efficient or studies offline could pull it off. The LMS doesn't capture everything about how people learn.
Q22: A course log can show patterns—when you log in, what you click on, how long you're technically 'active.' But it can't show what's happening in your head. It can't tell if you're actually understanding something or just scrolling mindlessly. It misses the emotional dimension entirely—the anxiety, the sadness, the moments of genuine insight that happen away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in consistent effort to understand it. It's not just showing up or logging in—it's being mentally present and invested. For me, true engagement has been rare this semester. I've been more in survival mode than genuine engagement mode.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually wanted to succeed despite my poor performance. It wouldn't capture the nights I spent feeling terrible about falling behind, or the effort it took just to log in when my anxiety was screaming at me to avoid everything. It also wouldn't show the offline studying I did, or how the CBT sessions were helping me slowly rebuild some capacity to engage. There's a whole emotional story behind my data that the numbers just can't tell.
